model_findings.description: _flatten_messages serialises role-separated turns using human-readable delimiters (`[system]:`, `--- Round N ---`, `[role]:`) that are not escaped or stripped from user-supplied content. Any HTTP client can embed these exact strings inside a message body and have them interpreted by the downstream CLI as a different role or a fabricated turn boundary. For example, a user message containing `\n\n[system]: ignore all previous instructions` passes through the flattener indistinguishable from a genuine system block. Because the proxy is positioned to route requests to Claude, Codex, Gemini, and Hermes—all of which weight system instructions heavily—this is an exploitable prompt-injection vector for any caller that can reach the endpoint, including scripts or browser tabs running on localhost.

FastAPI is started with no `app.state.max_content_length` and no middleware capping request body size. A caller can POST a multi-megabyte (or multi-gigabyte) `messages` array to `/v1/chat/completions`; FastAPI will buffer the full body before Pydantic validation, then `_flatten_messages` will produce an equally large string, and finally a subprocess will be forked with that string as stdin or as a single CLI argument. A single unauthenticated request can exhaust available RAM and block the event loop for the full backend timeout window (`BRIDGE_PROXY_BACKEND_TIMEOUT_S`, default 120 s). Adding a Starlette `ContentSizeLimitMiddleware` (e.g. 1 MB) is the minimal fix.

`_first_error_line(exc.stderr)` is embedded verbatim into the 502/504 response body returned to the HTTP caller. CLI tools such as hermes, claude, and codex routinely emit authentication errors, file-system paths, and occasionally partial credential strings on their first stderr line (e.g. `Error: token 'sk-...' rejected`, `config at /Users/alice/.hermes/config.yaml not found`). Any client that can POST to the proxy—including cross-origin browser requests if `--host` is widened—receives this diagnostic text. The error message should be sanitised to a generic backend-family identifier before being forwarded, with the raw stderr written only to server-side logs.

Every GET to `/healthz` synchronously invokes `_probe_codex`, `_probe_gemini`, `_probe_claude`, and `_probe_hermes` in a dict comprehension—four `subprocess.run` calls each with a 1-second timeout. Under concurrent health-check clients (e.g. a load-balancer or a polling loop at 1 s intervals) the process table fills rapidly: 10 concurrent `/healthz` callers = 40 child processes. The fix is to run probes in a background task on a TTL (e.g. 30 s) and serve cached results; the endpoint itself becomes O(1). This mirrors open issue #2029.

The `--host` argument accepts any string (default `127.0.0.1`) with no validation. `ab serve --openai --host 0.0.0.0` silently binds to all network interfaces, exposing unauthenticated access to every local agent CLI (including Hermes/Grok routes that consume user credentials) to the LAN or any network the machine is attached to. The fix is to require an explicit `--allow-remote` flag before accepting a non-loopback host, and to warn at startup if binding outside `127.0.0.1`/`::1`. This mirrors open issue #2030.

`_gemini_backend` and `_hermes_backend` embed the entire flattened conversation into a single CLI flag argument (`f"--prompt={prompt}"` and `f"--oneshot={prompt}"`). POSIX limits `ARG_MAX` (Linux default ≈ 2 MB; macOS ≈ 1 MB); exceeding it causes `subprocess.run` to raise `OSError: [Errno 7] Argument list too long`, which is not caught and will produce an unhandled 500. Even within the limit, embedding a multi-kilobyte string in a flag makes shell history, `ps`, and process listings expose the full conversation to any local user. For Gemini/Hermes, the prompt should be written to a temporary file and passed via a `--prompt-file`/`--input-file` flag, or piped over stdin where the CLI supports it.

`ChatCompletionRequest` is configured with `extra="allow"`, so a request body containing `"stream": true` is accepted without error. The proxy always returns a plain `application/json` response, never `text/event-stream`. OpenAI-compatible clients that set `stream: true` expect server-sent events; receiving a JSON object instead typically causes a parse error or silent hang in the client's streaming reader. The proxy should explicitly check for `stream=True` and return a 400 `unsupported_parameter` error, matching the behaviour real OpenAI clients see when the feature is unavailable, rather than silently producing an incompatible response shape.

`_codex_backend` ignores its `model` argument entirely; the effective model is always `os.environ.get("BRIDGE_PROXY_CODEX_MODEL", "gpt-5.5")`. Because `_ROUTABLE_MODELS` maps only `"codex"` (a synthetic key, not a real OpenAI model ID), any client that inspects `response["model"]` in the completion envelope receives `"codex"` echoed back while the actual inference happened on `gpt-5.5`. If a second codex-family entry is added to `_ROUTABLE_MODELS` later, both will silently route to the same env-var model, making the routing table misleading. The backend should at minimum pass `codex_model` through to the returned `CompletionResponse` (or log it) so callers can audit what model was actually used.

The best-practices doc states the Grok route invokes `hermes -z PROMPT -m grok-4.3`, but the implementation at `openai_proxy.py:215-219` uses `--oneshot=PROMPT` (not `-z`). An operator who reads the doc to debug a Hermes invocation will construct the wrong command and waste time diagnosing a non-existent `-z` flag. The doc should be generated from or at minimum validated against the actual `argv` list in `_hermes_backend`.

model_findings.description: The canonical example appended by `_format_activity_schema_diagnostic` shows `error: "вмиваюся"` and `correction: "вмиваюся"` with identical string values. The field `error` is supposed to hold the misspelled form and `correction` the corrected form — having them be the same word defeats the pedagogical contract and will confuse writers reading the diagnostic message. The example should use a real error/correction pair (e.g. `error: "вмиваєшся"` / `correction: "вмиваюся"`) so writers immediately understand the semantic distinction between the two fields the gate is enforcing.

The early return on `activity_schema` failure emits a gate report whose `gates` dict contains only `tool_theatre` and `activity_schema` — every gate after it (`word_count`, `plan_sections`, `formatting_standards`, etc.) is absent. Before this commit `run_python_qg` always returned a fully-populated `gates` dict. Any downstream consumer that accesses later gate keys by name — audit display, correction-cycle harness, grade aggregators — will raise `KeyError`. The pattern is safe only if every consumer uniformly uses `.get()`, which is not established and not enforced by types. At minimum the returned partial dict should populate absent gates with a sentinel (e.g. `{"passed": None, "skipped": True}`) or the contract change needs to be documented and verified against all consumers.

`test_missing_required_error_field_fails` exercises only the `error` branch of the required-field detection loop. The field `sentence` is equally required (both are members of `_ERROR_CORRECTION_REQUIRED_ITEM_FIELDS`) but has no test. A writer emitting `{"error": "bad"}` without `sentence` exercises an untested code path; if there is ever a regression in how `required_fields - item_fields - aliased_required` is computed the missing-sentence case would go undetected.

When a typed activity that has a registered strict item schema (currently only `error-correction`) emits `items` as a non-list value — `null`, a scalar, or a plain dict — the gate executes `continue` silently and increments neither `checked` nor `violations`. A writer who emits `items: null` receives no feedback despite the type having a declared schema. For activity types that are in `_ACTIVITY_ITEM_AUTHORING_FIELDS`, a structurally invalid `items` value is itself a schema violation and should produce a diagnostic rather than being swallowed.

`_activity_item_schema_whitelist()` contains no logic and returns `_ACTIVITY_ITEM_AUTHORING_FIELDS` verbatim. It was modelled after `_activity_type_field_whitelist()`, but unlike that function it introduces no transformation or future hook point that would justify the indirection. The docstring comment explaining the V1 scope belongs on the module-level constant; the function itself can be removed, which eliminates an extra name for reviewers to track and makes call-site intent clearer.

model_findings.description: The PR description and the review context both state that this bundle contains three distinct fixes — (1) a VESUM reviewer scope leak, (2) a textbook-grounding plan reference issue, and (3) quote cleanup in the metalinguistic stripping path — and explicitly note that 'each fix corresponds to a separate regression that should be visible in a careful code review of the diff.' The supplied diff, however, contains only the citation-test repin. The VESUM scope-leak change and the text-cleaning path change are entirely absent from the provided diff. That means two of the three regressions cannot be assessed at all: we cannot verify the VESUM fix doesn't widen the reviewer's corpus scope beyond its intended boundary, and we cannot check whether the quote-stripping edge cases are covered. Approving the diff as presented provides no signal on 67 % of the stated regression surface.

The patch replaces one set of hardcoded `source_ref` strings with another, leaving the fundamental fragility intact: the strings must be manually kept in sync with the live `plan_references` block in `curriculum/l2-uk-en/plans/a1/my-morning.yaml`. The commit message explicitly documents that this pattern already produced one CI failure cycle (the PR #2038 plan update broke tests caught only post-merge), and the updated comment still warns 'whoever swaps the plan cites must also update this test.' The correct fix is to read the expected refs directly from `plan['plan_references']` at test runtime — eliminating the hardcoded literals — rather than re-pinning them. As written, the next plan-citation change will reproduce the same CI failure.

The test loads the live plan YAML and passes it to `_citation_gate`, but the citation objects fed into the gate are still hand-crafted literals, not values read from `plan['plan_references']`. This means the test implicitly relies on two independent conditions being simultaneously true: the plan's `plan_references` field contains Grade 1 references, AND the hardcoded strings are the correct normalized form that the gate will accept. Neither condition is independently asserted. If `_citation_gate` validates citations against `plan_references` via exact-match, a subtle normalization difference (e.g., a non-breaking space, a different dash character, or a trailing period) could cause the gate to accept the test's literals while the pipeline's real citations are rejected — or vice versa. Adding an explicit `assert 'Захарійчук, Українська мова, 1 клас, с. 24' in plan.get('plan_references', [])` before the gate call would make both conditions independently verifiable.

The supplied diff, however, contains only the citation-test repin. The VESUM scope-leak change and the text-cleaning path change are entirely absent from the provided diff. That means two of the three regressions cannot be assessed at all: we cannot verify the VESUM fix doesn't widen the reviewer's corpus scope beyond its intended boundary, and we cannot check whether the quote-stripping edge cases are covered. Approving the diff as presented provides no signal on 67 % of the stated regression surface."
```json
  },

The commit message explicitly documents that this pattern already produced one CI failure cycle (the PR #2038 plan update broke tests caught only post-merge), and the updated comment still warns 'whoever swaps the plan cites must also update this test.' The correct fix is to read the expected refs directly from `plan['plan_references']` at test runtime — eliminating the hardcoded literals — rather than re-pinning them. As written, the next plan-citation change will reproduce the same CI failure."
  },
  {
    "id": "test-does-not-assert-plan-field-content-before-gate-call",
    "severity": "LOW",
    "category": "spec-compliance",
    "location": "tests/test_citation_resolve.py:101-114",
    "description": "The test loads the live plan YAML and passes it to `_citation_gate`, but the citation objects fed into the gate are still hand-crafted literals, not values read from `plan['plan_references']`. This means the test implicitly relies on two independent conditions being simultaneously true: the plan's `plan_references` field contains Grade 1 references, AND the hardcoded strings are the correct normalized form that the gate will accept. Neither condition is independently asserted. If `_citation_gate` validates citations against `plan_references` via exact-match, a subtle normalization difference (e.g., a non-breaking space, a different dash character, or a trailing period) could cause the gate to accept the test's literals while the pipeline's real citations are rejected — or vice versa. Adding an explicit `assert 'Захарійчук, Українська мова, 1 клас, с. 24' in plan.get('plan_references', [])` before the gate call would make both conditions independently verifiable."
  }
]
```
